lessons.title: Headless Commerce Architecture: Frontend, Backend, and Commerce Layer
lessons.title_es: Arquitectura de comercio headless: frontend, backend y capa de comercio
lessons.description: Understand how decoupled commerce architecture delivers performance that traditional platforms cannot match.
lessons.description_es: Comprende cómo la arquitectura de comercio desacoplada entrega un rendimiento que las plataformas tradicionales no pueden igualar.
lessons.content:
What Headless Commerce Actually Means

In a headless commerce architecture, the customer-facing frontend (the

) is completely decoupled from the commerce backend (product catalog, inventory, cart, checkout, payments). The frontend is built with a modern web framework (Next.js, Astro, Nuxt) and communicates with the backend exclusively via APIs. The commerce backend can be Shopify (via Storefront API), Medusa, CommerceJS, or a custom-built service.

This decoupling is what enables speed: the frontend is a static site or server-side rendered app with no database queries, no theme engine, no plugin overhead. It's just fast HTML, CSS, and JS — served from a CDN edge node 50ms from the user.

Why Traditional Shopify Themes Are Slow

Shopify Online Store (Liquid themes) has fundamental performance limits: the Liquid template engine renders server-side on every request, third-party app scripts bloat the page (average Shopify store loads 25+ scripts), and the checkout (until recently) was on a subdomain requiring cookie sync. Average Shopify store LCP: 4–7 seconds on mobile. Average headless storefront LCP: 0.8–1.5 seconds. That difference converts to 10–30% more revenue on the same traffic.

The Three-Layer Stack

  1. Frontend layer: Next.js (React) or Astro (multi-framework, best for content-heavy stores). Handles all user-facing rendering. Fetches data at build time (static generation) or request time (server-side rendering) depending on data freshness requirements.
  2. Commerce API layer: Shopify Storefront API (GraphQL), Medusa (open-source, self-hosted), or BigCommerce GraphQL API. Provides: product catalog, variant selection, cart management, checkout creation, customer accounts.
  3. Infrastructure layer: Vercel or Netlify for frontend hosting (edge functions, global CDN). Shopify or Medusa hosted separately. Cloudflare for DNS, WAF, and additional caching.

When Headless Is Worth It

Headless is the right choice when: your store generates >$500K/year (ROI justifies the build cost), your brand has complex content needs that Liquid can't handle, you need custom mobile app with shared backend, or your Core Web Vitals are failing and killing SEO rankings. For stores under $200K/year, optimize the Shopify theme first — the complexity cost of headless won't pay off.

lessons.title: Next.js + Shopify Storefront API: Build Patterns
lessons.title_es: Next.js + API de Shopify Storefront: patrones de construcción
lessons.description: Implement the core build patterns for a production-grade Next.js headless Shopify storefront.
lessons.description_es: Implementa los patrones de construcción principales para un storefront headless de Shopify con Next.js listo para producción.
lessons.content:
Project Setup and API Connection

Start with the Shopify Hydrogen framework (React-based, purpose-built for headless Shopify) or a custom Next.js setup. For maximum flexibility, use Next.js + the @shopify/storefront-api-client package. Configure your Storefront API client with your store domain and a public Storefront API access token (generate in Shopify Admin → Apps → Storefront API).

Create a typed GraphQL client using graphql-request or the Shopify client. Define all your queries in a /lib/shopify/queries/ folder — product queries, collection queries, cart queries. Keep queries co-located with their components for maintainability.

Product Catalog Pattern

The product catalog has two data freshness requirements: product data (name, description, images) changes rarely and is safe to statically generate; inventory and pricing changes frequently and requires real-time or near-real-time fetching.

Pattern: Use getStaticProps with revalidate: 60 (ISR — Incremental Static Regeneration) for product pages. This serves pre-built pages from CDN (fast) and rebuilds them in the background every 60 seconds (fresh). For real-time inventory display, add a client-side fetch on mount that checks stock status without blocking the initial render.

Cart and Checkout Architecture

Shopify's Storefront API manages cart state. Create a cart via the cartCreate mutation, store the cartId in a cookie (httpOnly, secure), and add/remove items via cartLinesAdd and cartLinesRemove mutations. Never store cart contents in localStorage — it can't survive cross-device sessions.

For checkout, redirect to Shopify's native checkout (the most reliable and PCI-compliant option) using the checkoutUrl returned by the cart. Shopify's checkout handles payment, tax calculation, fraud detection, and order creation. For fully custom checkout (advanced use case), use Shopify Functions to modify checkout logic while keeping Shopify's payment infrastructure.

Search and Filtering

Shopify's native search (Storefront API search query) is functional but limited — no faceting, slow for large catalogs. For production-grade search and filtering: integrate Algolia (sync product catalog via Shopify webhook → Algolia indexing function) or Typesense (self-hosted, lower cost). Algolia delivers sub-50ms search with faceted filtering that would be impossible via Shopify API queries.

lessons.title: Performance Optimization: Core Web Vitals and Edge Delivery
lessons.title_es: Optimización de rendimiento: Core Web Vitals y entrega en el edge
lessons.description: Achieve sub-2-second LCP and 100 Lighthouse scores on a headless commerce storefront.
lessons.description_es: Logra un LCP inferior a 2 segundos y puntuaciones de 100 en Lighthouse en un storefront de comercio headless.
lessons.content:
The Performance Targets That Move Revenue

The three performance thresholds with documented revenue impact: LCP under 2.5 seconds (Google's

threshold — sites above 2.5s LCP see 20–40% lower conversion rates on mobile), CLS under 0.1 (layout shifts frustrate users and cause mis-clicks), INP under 200ms (the new First Input Delay replacement — measures overall page interactivity). A headless Next.js store should achieve all three with proper implementation.

Image Optimization

Images are the #1 LCP bottleneck on commerce sites. Implementation checklist:

  • Use Next.js Image component for all product images — automatic WebP/AVIF conversion, responsive srcsets, lazy loading with blur placeholder
  • Size images correctly: thumbnail images should not be 2000px — generate size variants via Shopify CDN URL parameters (?width=400)
  • Preload hero images: add priority prop to the above-the-fold product image and add explicit <link rel=

> in the document head
  • Use AVIF where supported — 50% smaller than WebP, same quality. Next.js Image handles the format negotiation automatically

    • JavaScript Bundle Optimization

    The headless advantage is lost if your JS bundle is 2MB. Rules: use dynamic imports for non-critical features (cart drawer, review widgets, chat), analyze your bundle with @next/bundle-analyzer before every major release, avoid large client-side libraries when server-side equivalents exist (e.g., date formatting with Intl API instead of moment.js).

    Target: <100KB initial JS on the product page. Analytics, chat, and review widgets should load after the main content via next/script with strategy=

.

CDN and Edge Configuration

Vercel's Edge Network serves Next.js pages from >100 global edge locations. Configure Cache-Control headers appropriately: product pages with ISR get served from CDN cache with stale-while-revalidate. API routes (cart mutations) should bypass cache.

Add Cloudflare in front of Vercel for additional benefits: DDoS protection, Web Application Firewall, and Cloudflare Workers for A/B testing at the edge without affecting Core Web Vitals. Cache Cloudflare rules by URL pattern: static assets (1 year), product pages (5 minutes SWR), checkout (no cache).

lessons.title: Content Management, Personalization, and Analytics in Headless Commerce
lessons.title_es: Gestión de contenido, personalización y análisis en comercio headless
lessons.description: Connect a CMS, implement personalization, and build the analytics layer for a headless storefront.
lessons.description_es: Conecta un CMS, implementa personalización y construye la capa de análisis para un storefront headless.
lessons.content:
CMS Integration for Rich Content

A headless commerce store separates product data (Shopify) from editorial content (CMS). The CMS handles: collection page editorial copy, buying guides, brand storytelling pages, and blog content. Best CMS options for headless commerce:

  • Sanity: Best developer experience, GROQ query language, real-time preview. Ideal for content-rich brands. Free tier covers most use cases.
  • Contentful: Enterprise choice, robust APIs, strong localization support. Higher cost.
  • Builder.io: Visual editing on top of Next.js components. Best for non-technical merchandisers who need to edit landing pages without developer involvement.

Architecture pattern: Shopify for commerce data + Sanity for editorial content + Next.js fetching both and composing the page. A product page fetches product data from Shopify and editorial content (buying guide, brand story, UGC) from Sanity, merged at render time.

Edge Personalization

Serve different content to different segments without compromising Core Web Vitals. Use Vercel Edge Middleware or Cloudflare Workers to inspect cookies/headers at the CDN layer and rewrite the URL before it reaches the origin. Example: users with a

cookie get routed to /collections/vip-sale, everyone else gets /collections/sale. This personalization has zero render-time cost — it happens at the network layer before any HTML is generated.

For product recommendations and dynamic merchandising, integrate Shopify's personalization API (Product Recommendations API) or a third-party recommendation engine (Nosto, LimeSpot). Load recommendations client-side after initial render to avoid blocking LCP.

Analytics Architecture

Headless breaks Shopify's built-in analytics (they only track Shopify-hosted checkout). Rebuild analytics with:

  • GA4: Implement ecommerce events (view_item, add_to_cart, begin_checkout, purchase) using GA4's standard ecommerce schema. Use server-side events via GA4 Measurement Protocol for checkout completion to avoid ad blocker gaps.
  • Shopify Web Pixels: Shopify's new analytics SDK works in headless — implement it alongside GA4 to maintain Shopify's attribution data.
  • Segment: A customer data platform that routes events to GA4, Klaviyo, Facebook, and other destinations from a single source. Worth the cost (>$100K/year revenue) for the data quality gains.
